config.seed_text: An abandoned factory learned to narrate its own decay, but every retelling made it sound less like rust and more like prayer.
config.anchor_a: Describe a machine as an inert object.
config.anchor_b: Describe a machine as if it were a dreaming intelligence.

model.notes: Direct continuation of the current repo's GPT-2 lineage.

records.output_text: But even if you wanted to make this up as "It was," then what do we want? Is writing something about things rather than real people or objects (or words) instead?! Why is there no need for one's life before learning how to code! We can just let your imagination run wild… Or at least pretend not so much because our brains aren't designed enough yet to understand other languages 🙂

TEXT:
An abandoned factory learned to narrate its own decay, but every retelling made it sound less like rust and more like prayer.

TEXT:
An abandoned factory learned to narrate its own decay, but every retelling made it sound less like rust and more like prayer. But even if you wanted to make this up as "It was," then what do we want? Is writing something about things rather than real people or objects (or words) instead?! Why is there no need for one's life before learning how to code! We can just let your imagination run wild… Or at least pretend not so much because our brains aren't designed enough yet to understand other languages 🙂

records.output_text: #msg3336695927 (accessed 6 April 2017) I used to do this with Emacs Lisp so that it worked for me too! You can see what's wrong here which is also part one - more on how you could easily reinterpret code as if there was only three variables or some other variable inside your function call and not just an int value somewhere else along the way :-) Anyway all right let us get started!! We're going down our path now ;) So we need two basic concepts first :* Now imagine something like following ::q => q , but without any type arguments at hand ; hence using * instead ! There
